ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311171 'milk tea' is the closest parent class. No specific entry exists for wildflower honey green milk tea or KOI-style nai lü in FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP 'Tea-based beverages' is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for honey green milk tea in FoodEx2 as of April 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34111008 'Tea (substance)' is the closest available concept. No specific concept for milk tea with honey exists in SNOMED CT as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of wildflower honey green milk tea as served at KOI Thé Singapore, approximately 500 ml, containing brewed green tea, fresh milk, and wildflower honey.

nutrient_source.title: Milk Tea, ready-to-drink, estimated from green tea base with whole milk and honey components; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed green tea (FDC ID 171917), whole milk (FDC ID 171265), and honey (FDC ID 169640)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for wildflower honey green milk tea exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries: brewed green tea (~60% by volume), whole milk (~35% by volume), and honey (~5% by volume), calibrated to a typical KOI large-size serving composition. Energy and macros are consistent with published analyses of similar Taiwanese milk tea beverages in peer-reviewed literature (Huang et al., 2021, Food Chemistry). Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of honey and milk tea components; honey GI ~55 (Atkinson et al., 2008); whole milk GI ~27 (Foster-Powell et al., 2002); green tea GI negligible; composite estimate weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journals (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wildflower honey green milk tea. The composite GI of 55 is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted average method (FAO/WHO 1998): honey contributes the dominant glycemic carbohydrate load (GI ~55 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, AJCN 88:1, 5–56S); whole milk contributes minor carbohydrate with GI ~27 (Foster-Powell et al. 2002, AJCN 76:1, 5–56); green tea contributes negligible carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI ≈ 55. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 44 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.2. A GI of 55 is classified as 'low'.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~90 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 18 mg sodium per 100 g (90 mg per large serving), this beverage poses minimal sodium burden for individuals managing hypertension, though regular consumption of sweetened beverages should be monitored for overall dietary pattern effects.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content from wildflower honey (~41 g per 500 ml serving) with low glycemic index (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this beverage, as the wildflower honey contributes approximately 41 g of free sugars per large serving with a composite glycemic index of ~55 (classified as low), producing a glycemic load of ~24, which can still cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction or sugar-free substitution is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from milk (~4.5 g per 500 ml serving) and high free sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from whole milk (~4.5 g per serving) and high free sugar content (~41 g per serving) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as chronic high sugar intake is associated with elevated triglycerides and adverse lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wildflower Honey Green Milk Tea (Large) — known in Chinese as 野花蜜奶绿 (大) — is a Taiwanese-style milk tea beverage served at KOI Thé, a popular Taiwanese tea chain with outlets across Singapore. The drink is prepared by brewing high-quality green tea, blending it with fresh milk to create a smooth, creamy base known as 'nai lü' (奶绿), and sweetening it with wildflower honey rather than conventional sugar syrup. The result is a lightly floral, mildly sweet beverage with a delicate grassy green tea character balanced by the natural complexity of wildflower honey and the creaminess of milk. It is typically served cold or over ice. Nutritionally, a large 500 ml serving contains approximately 260 kcal, 44 g of carbohydrates (predominantly free sugars from honey), 7 g of fat, and 6 g of protein. The composite glycemic index is estimated at ~55 (low), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving — clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The beverage is a source of calcium (~210 mg per serving) from milk. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Individuals with diabetes, obesity, or cardiovascular risk should be aware of the high free sugar content contributed by the wildflower honey. This beverage is a culturally significant item in Singapore's Taiwanese tea culture and is commonly consumed as an afternoon refreshment or social drink.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for wildflower honey green milk tea exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed green tea, whole milk, and honey, calibrated to a typical KOI large-size serving composition. GI is a carbohydrate-weighted composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for honey and milk. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (brewed green tea FDC ID 171917, whole milk FDC ID 171265, honey FDC ID 169640) weighted by approximate volumetric composition of a KOI large serving. Exact formulation is proprietary to KOI Thé and not publicly disclosed. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: green tea broth and honey contribute negligible dietary fibre; milk contains no fibre. This is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source entries. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero for this beverage composition; no hydrogenated fats are used. 4. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component source entries for this beverage blend. 5. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 6. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 11. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 12. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the blended source entries. 13. GI of 55 is a carbohydrate-weighted composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 14. This record is classified as Taiwanese cuisine; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual rules, as this beverage is served in Singapore. 15. Serving size of 500 g is an approximation for a large (大) cup; actual fill volume may vary by outlet.
